results.possibleIdentity: Bruce Alan MacCurdy (1944–1987) was an animator, filmmaker, and educator active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily based in Syracuse, New York. He taught filmmaking and animation at Syracuse University for ten years and was a significant figure in the experimental video and film scene of the era. His work is associated with the early development of video art; in 1975, he participated in the 'Community Video' exhibition at the Everson Museum of Art in Syracuse, where his collaborations with Owen Shapiro were featured alongside the work of video art pioneers such as Bill Viola and Frank Gillette. MacCurdy was also a mentor to several notable artists, including director Henry Selick, and was a close associate of underground cartoonist Vaughn Bodē, with whom he explored the intersection of animation and underground comics. His film 'Flipping' has been cited in research concerning the evolution of animation storyboard styles and graphic design systems. He worked as a self-employed filmmaker producing television commercials as well as films for public television and Colgate University.

results.possibleIdentity: Jill Rosenberg is an artist and administrator who was notably active in the late 1960s and early 1970s within the New York and international avant-garde art scenes. She is primarily associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the influential non-profit organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaboration between artists and engineers. Rosenberg served as the Office Manager for E.A.T. in New York and played a significant role in the administration and coordination of the organization's projects, including the development of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. Her work often intersected with performance and media art; she is listed in the archives of the Western Front, an artist-run center in Vancouver, for her participation in various media and performance events, such as "Hank’s Industrial Cabaret" in 1989, where she performed alongside artists like Tony Oursler and Jim Shaw. Her career spans early administrative support for technological art experiments to later performance-based activities.
